The processing of photographs should not systemati­ cally  be  considered to be processing of special categories of personal data as they are covered by the definition of biometric  data  only  when  processed  through  a  specific  technical  means  allowing  the  unique  identification  or authentication  of  a  natural  person.  Such  personal  data  should  not  be  processed,  unless  processing  is  allowed  in specific  cases  set  out  in  this  Regulation,  taking  into  account  that  Member  States  law  may  lay  down  specific provisions  on  data  protection  in  order  to  adapt  the  application  of  the  rules  of  this  Regulation  for  compliance with  a  legal  obligation  or  for  the  performance  of  a  task  carried  out  in  the  public  interest  or  in  the  exercise  of official  authority  vested in  the  controller.  In  addition  to the  specific  requirements  for  such processing,  the  general principles  and  other  rules  of  this  Regulation  should  apply,  in  particular  as  regards  the  conditions  for  lawful processing.

## Article 87

## Processing of the national identification number

Member  States  may  further  determine  the  specific  conditions  for  the  processing  of  a  national  identification  number  or any  other  identifier  of  general  application.  In  that  case  the  national  identification  number  or  any  other  identifier  of general  application  shall  be  used  only  under  appropriate  safeguards  for  the  rights  and  freedoms  of  the  data  subject pursuant to this Regulation.

## Article 88

## Processing in the context of employment

1. Member States may, by law or by collective agreements, provide for more specific rules to ensure the protection of the  rights  and  freedoms  in  respect  of  the  processing  of  employees'  personal  data  in  the  employment  context,  in particular  for  the  purposes  of  the  recruitment,  the  performance  of  the  contract  of  employment,  including  discharge  of obligations laid down by law or by collective agreements, management, planning and organisation of work, equality and diversity  in  the  workplace,  health  and  safety  at  work,  protection  of  employer's  or  customer's  property  and  for  the purposes  of the exercise  and  enjoyment,  on  an  individual  or collective basis, of rights and  benefits related to employment, and for the purpose of the termination of the employment relationship.
2. Those  rules  shall  include  suitable  and  specific  measures  to  safeguard  the  data  subject's  human  dignity,  legitimate interests  and  fundamental  rights,  with  particular  regard  to  the  transparency  of  processing,  the  transfer  of  personal  data within  a  group  of  undertakings,  or  a  group  of  enterprises  engaged  in  a  joint  economic  activity  and  monitoring  systems at the work place.
3. Each  Member  State  shall  notify  to  the  Commission  those  provisions  of  its  law  which  it  adopts  pursuant  to paragraph 1, by 25 May 2018 and, without delay, any subsequent amendment affecting them.

## Article 89

5. To the extent that it is strictly necessary for  the purpose of ensuring bias detection and correction in relation to the high-risk AI systems in accordance with paragraph (2), points (f) and (g) of this Article, the providers of such systems may exceptionally process special categories of personal data, subject to appropriate safeguards for  the fundamental rights and freedoms of natural persons. In addition to the provisions set out in Regulations (EU) 2016/679 and (EU) 2018/1725 and Directive  (EU)  2016/680,  all  the  following  conditions  must  be  met  in  order  for  such  processing  to  occur: 2. (a) the  bias  detection  and  correction  cannot  be  effectively  fulfilled  by  processing  other  data,  including  synthetic  or anonymised data; 3. (b) the  special  categories  of  personal  data  are  subject  to  technical  limitations  on  the  re-use  of  the  personal  data,  and state-of-the-art  security  and  privacy-preserving  measures,  including  pseudonymisation; 4. (c) the special categories of personal data are subject to measures to ensure that the personal data processed are secured, protected, subject to suitable safeguards, including strict controls and documentation of the access, to avoid misuse and ensure  that  only  authorised  persons  have  access  to  those  personal  data  with  appropriate  confidentiality obligations; 5. (d) the  special  categories  of  personal  data  are  not  to  be  transmitted,  transferred  or  otherwise  accessed  by other  parties; 6. (e) the special categories of personal data are deleted once the bias has been corrected or the personal data has reached the end  of  its  retention  period,  whichever  comes  first; 7. (f) the  records  of  processing  activities  pursuant  to  Regulations  (EU)  2016/679  and  (EU)  2018/1725  and  Directive  (EU) 2016/680 include the reasons why the processing of special categories of personal data was strictly necessary to detect and correct  biases,  and  why that  objective  could  not  be  achieved  by  processing  other  data. 6.

Personal  data  should  be  processed  in  a manner  that  ensures  appropriate  security  and  confidentiality  of  the  personal  data,  including  for  preventing unauthorised access to or use of personal data and the equipment used for the processing.

- (40) In  order  for  processing  to  be  lawful,  personal  data  should  be  processed  on  the  basis  of  the  consent  of  the  data subject concerned or some other legitimate basis, laid down by law, either in this Regulation or in other Union or

Member  State  law  as  referred  to  in  this  Regulation,  including  the  necessity  for  compliance  with  the  legal obligation  to which the  controller  is  subject  or  the  necessity  for  the  performance  of  a  contract  to which  the  data subject is party or in order  to take steps at the request of  the data subject prior  to entering into a contract.

- (e)   kept  in  a  form  which  permits  identification  of  data  subjects  for  no  longer  than  is  necessary  for  the  purposes  for which  the  personal  data  are  processed;  personal  data  may  be  stored  for  longer  periods  insofar  as  the  personal  data will  be  processed  solely  for  archiving  purposes  in  the  public  interest,  scientific  or  historical  research  purposes  or statistical  purposes  in  accordance  with  Article  89(1)  subject  to  implementation  of  the  appropriate  technical  and organisational measures required by this Regulation in order  to safeguard the rights and freedoms of the data subject ('storage limitation');
- (f)   processed in a manner  that  ensures appropriate security of the personal data, including protection against unauthorised  or  unlawful  processing  and  against  accidental  loss,  destruction  or  damage,  using  appropriate  technical or organisational measures ('integrity and confidentiality').
2. The controller shall be responsible for, and be able to demonstrate compliance with, paragraph 1 ('accountability').

## Article 6

## Lawfulness of processing

The principle of transparency requires that any information and communi­ cation relating to the processing of  those personal data be easily accessible and easy to understand, and that clear and  plain  language  be  used.  That  principle  concerns,  in  particular,  information  to  the  data  subjects  on  the identity  of the  controller  and  the  purposes  of the  processing  and  further  information  to  ensure  fair  and transparent  processing  in  respect  of  the  natural  persons  concerned  and  their  right  to  obtain  confirmation  and communication  of  personal  data  concerning  them  which  are  being  processed.  Natural  persons  should  be  made aware of risks, rules, safeguards and rights in relation to the processing of personal data and how to exercise their rights  in  relation  to  such  processing.  In  particular,  the  specific  purposes  for  which  personal  data  are  processed should  be  explicit  and  legitimate  and  determined  at  the  time  of  the  collection  of  the  personal  data.  The  personal data should be adequate, relevant and limited to what is necessary for  the purposes for  which they are processed. This  requires,  in  particular,  ensuring  that  the  period  for  which  the  personal  data  are  stored  is  limited  to  a  strict minimum.  Personal  data  should  be  processed  only  if  the  purpose  of  the  processing  could  not  reasonably  be fulfilled  by other  means.  In  order  to  ensure  that  the  personal  data  are  not  kept  longer  than  necessary,  time  limits should be established by the controller for erasure or for a periodic review. Every reasonable step should be taken to  ensure  that  personal  data  which  are  inaccurate  are  rectified  or  deleted.  Personal  data  should  be  processed  in  a manner  that  ensures  appropriate  security  and  confidentiality  of  the  personal  data,  including  for  preventing unauthorised access to or use of personal data and the equipment used for the processing.

## Article 25

## Data protection by design and by default

1. Taking into account the state of the art, the cost of implementation and the nature, scope, context and purposes of processing as well as the risks of varying likelihood and severity for  rights and freedoms of natural persons posed by the processing, the controller shall, both at the time of the determination of  the means for processing and at the time of  the processing  itself,  implement  appropriate  technical  and  organisational  measures,  such  as  pseudonymisation,  which  are designed to implement data-protection principles, such as data minimisation, in an effective manner and to integrate the necessary  safeguards  into  the  processing  in  order  to  meet  the  requirements  of  this  Regulation  and  protect  the  rights  of data subjects.
2. The  controller  shall  implement  appropriate  technical  and  organisational  measures  for  ensuring  that,  by  default, only personal data which are necessary for each specific purpose of the processing are processed. That obligation applies to the amount of personal data collected, the extent of their processing, the period of their storage and their accessibility. In  particular,  such  measures  shall  ensure  that  by  default  personal  data  are  not  made  accessible  without  the  individual's intervention to an indefinite number of natural persons.
3. An  approved  certification  mechanism  pursuant  to  Article  42  may  be  used  as  an  element  to  demonstrate compliance with the requirements set out in paragraphs 1 and 2 of this Article.

## Article 26

## Joint controllers

In  addition  to the  specific  requirements  for  such processing,  the  general principles  and  other  rules  of  this  Regulation  should  apply,  in  particular  as  regards  the  conditions  for  lawful processing.  Derogations  from  the  general  prohibition  for  processing  such  special  categories  of  personal  data should  be  explicitly  provided,  inter  alia,  where  the  data  subject  gives  his  or  her  explicit  consent  or  in  respect  of specific  needs  in  particular  where  the  processing  is  carried  out  in  the  course  of  legitimate  activities  by  certain associations or foundations the purpose of which is to permit the exercise of fundamental freedoms. - (52) Derogating  from  the  prohibition  on  processing  special  categories  of  personal  data  should  also  be  allowed  when provided for  in  Union or  Member State law and subject to suitable safeguards, so as to protect personal data and other  fundamental  rights,  where  it  is  in  the  public  interest  to  do  so,  in  particular  processing  personal  data  in  the field  of  employment  law,  social  protection  law  including  pensions  and  for  health  security,  monitoring  and  alert purposes,  the  prevention  or  control  of  communicable  diseases  and  other  serious  threats  to  health.  Such  a derogation  may  be  made  for  health  purposes,  including  public  health  and  the  management  of  health-care services,  especially  in  order  to  ensure  the  quality  and  cost-effectiveness  of  the  procedures  used  for  settling  claims for  benefits  and  services  in  the  health  insurance  system,  or  for  archiving  purposes  in  the  public  interest,  scientific or  historical  research  purposes  or  statistical  purposes.
results.output:
Based on the provided GDPR context, the relevant categories of personal data for the processing activity **"Supplier tenant management"** for the purpose **"Execution and management of contractually agreed deliveries or services using a supplier database or file"** would likely include the following, as inferred from the definitions and principles of GDPR:

### **Relevant Personal Data Categories:**
1. **Identifying Data** (Article 4(1) definition of personal data):
   - Names of supplier representatives or contacts.
   - Identification numbers (e.g., VAT numbers, tax IDs, or other business identifiers).
   - Contact details (e.g., email addresses, phone numbers, postal addresses).
   - Online identifiers (e.g., IP addresses, usernames, or supplier portal login credentials if used for contract management).

2. **Contractual and Business Data** (implied by the purpose of contract execution):
   - Supplier contract details (e.g., contract terms, delivery schedules, service-level agreements).
   - Financial data (e.g., bank account details for payments, invoicing information).
   - Performance metrics (e.g., delivery timelines, quality metrics, compliance records).

3. **Special Categories of Personal Data (if applicable)** (Article 9 and Recital 51):
   - **Not typically relevant** for standard supplier tenant management unless the processing involves:
     - Health data (e.g., if suppliers are required to disclose health/safety certifications for hazardous materials).
     - Biometric data (e.g., if suppliers use biometric authentication for access to restricted areas).
     - Data revealing racial/ethnic origin (unlikely unless explicitly required for diversity reporting).
   - If such data are processed, they would require explicit legal grounds (e.g., consent, contractual necessity, or public interest under Article 9(2)).

4. **Employment-Related Data (if suppliers are individuals)** (Article 88 employment context):
   - Employee names, roles, or contact details of supplier staff involved in contract execution.
   - Performance evaluations (if relevant to contract compliance).

5. **Technical and Organizational Data** (Article 30 records of processing activities):
   - Data on system access (e.g., who accessed the supplier database/file).
   - Security logs (if personal data are involved in audit trails).

---

### **What is Missing from the Context:**
1. **Explicit Legal Basis for Processing**:
   - The context does not specify whether the processing relies on **contractual necessity (Article 6(1)(b))**, **legitimate interests (Article 6(1)(f))**, or another lawful basis. This would depend on the controller’s assessment.
   - If special categories of data are processed, the context lacks details on **explicit consent (Article 9(2)(a))** or **public interest derogations (Article 9(2)(g))**.

2. **Data Subject Categories**:
   - The context does not clarify whether the suppliers are **natural persons (individuals)** or **legal entities (companies)**. If suppliers are companies, their personal data (e.g., contact persons) would still be covered, but the processing scope differs.

3. **Storage Limitation and Security Measures**:
   - The context does not specify **retention periods (Article 5(1)(e))** or **technical/organizational safeguards (Article 32)** for the supplier database/file.

4. **Transfers to Third Countries**:
   - If the supplier database/file includes transfers to non-EU suppliers, the context lacks details on **adequacy decisions or Standard Contractual Clauses (Article 46)**.

---
### **Conclusion:**
For **"Supplier tenant management"** under the purpose of **"Execution and management of contractually agreed deliveries or services"**, the relevant personal data categories are primarily:
- **Identifying data** (names, contact details, identifiers).
- **Contractual/business data** (contract terms, financial details, performance metrics).
- **Employment-related data** (if suppliers are individuals).
- **Technical/organizational data** (access logs, security records).

**Special categories of data** would only apply in exceptional cases (e.g., health/safety certifications) and would require additional safeguards under Article 9.

For full compliance, the controller must ensure:
1. A **lawful basis** (e.g., contractual necessity).
2. **Data minimization** (only necessary data are processed).
3. **Security measures** (Article 32).
4. **Transparency** (informing suppliers of processing purposes and rights).

results.output:
Based on the provided GDPR context, the categories of personal data relevant for the processing activity **"Data backup / backups"** for the purpose **"Securing data in backups to ensure data availability"** would depend on the **content of the backups themselves**.

### **Relevant GDPR Definitions & Principles:**
1. **Personal Data (Art. 4(1))**:
   - Any information relating to an **identified or identifiable natural person** (data subject).
   - An identifiable person is one who can be identified **directly or indirectly** (e.g., via name, ID, location data, online identifiers, or factors specific to their identity).

2. **Processing (Art. 4(2))**:
   - Includes **storage, backup, and retrieval** of personal data.
   - If the backup contains personal data (e.g., employee records, customer data, or any data that can identify individuals), it constitutes **processing under GDPR**.

3. **Storage Limitation (Art. 5(1)(e))**:
   - Personal data should not be kept longer than necessary.
   - If backups are kept for **archiving, scientific, historical, or statistical purposes**, they may be retained longer **if safeguards (Art. 89) are applied**.

4. **Integrity & Confidentiality (Art. 5(1)(f))**:
   - Backups must be secured against **unauthorized access, loss, or damage** (e.g., encryption, access controls).

5. **Lawful Basis for Processing (Art. 6)**:
   - If the backup is for **legitimate business continuity**, it may rely on:
     - **Contractual necessity** (e.g., ensuring service availability).
     - **Legal obligation** (e.g., financial record retention laws).
     - **Legitimate interests** (e.g., protecting business operations).

### **Potential Personal Data in Backups:**
If the backup contains any of the following, it is **personal data** under GDPR:
- **Employee data** (names, contact details, HR records).
- **Customer data** (names, emails, payment details).
- **User account information** (usernames, IP addresses, device IDs).
- **Biometric or health data** (if stored in backups).
- **Location or behavioral data** (if identifiable).

### **Missing Context (Not Provided in GDPR Text):**
- **Specific types of personal data** in the backup (e.g., whether it includes special categories like health or biometric data).
- **Retention periods** (how long backups are kept).
- **Security measures** (encryption, access controls).
- **Third-country transfers** (if backups are stored outside the EU).

### **Conclusion:**
The **processing activity "Data backup"** is relevant under GDPR **only if the backup contains personal data**. If it does, the controller must ensure:
✅ **Lawful basis** (Art. 6).
✅ **Security measures** (Art. 5(1)(f), Art. 32).
✅ **Storage limitation** (Art. 5(1)(e)).
✅ **Accountability** (Art. 5(2)).

If the backup contains **special categories of data** (e.g., health, biometric), stricter rules apply (Art. 9, 51).

Would you like further clarification on any specific aspect?

Personal or

( 1 ) Commission Recommendation of 6 May 2003 concerning the definition of micro, small and medium-sized enterprises (C(2003) 1422) (OJ L 124, 20.5.2003, p. 36).

( 2 ) Regulation (EC) No 45/2001 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 18 December 2000 on the protection of individuals with regard to the processing of personal data by the Community institutions and bodies and on the free movement of such data (OJ L 8, 12.1.2001, p. 1).

household activities  could  include  correspondence  and  the  holding  of  addresses,  or  social  networking  and  online activity  undertaken  within  the  context  of  such  activities.  However,  this  Regulation  applies  to  controllers  or processors which provide the means for processing personal data for such personal or household activities.

- adopted on the basis of Article 16 TFEU, or subject to their application, which relate to the processing of personal data  by  the  Member  States  when  carrying  out  activities  falling  within  the  scope  of  Chapter  4  or  Chapter  5  of Title  V of  Part  Three  of  the  TFEU. - (42) In  line  with  the  presumption  of  innocence,  natural  persons  in  the  Union  should  always  be  judged  on  their  actual behaviour.  Natural  persons  should  never  be  judged  on  AI-predicted  behaviour  based  solely  on  their  profiling, personality traits or characteristics, such as nationality, place of birth, place of residence, number of children, level of debt  or  type  of  car,  without  a  reasonable  suspicion  of  that  person  being  involved  in  a  criminal  activity  based  on objective verifiable facts and without human assessment thereof. Therefore, risk assessments carried out with regard to  natural  persons  in  order  to  assess  the  likelihood  of  their  offending  or  to  predict  the  occurrence  of  an  actual  or potential criminal offence based solely on profiling them or on assessing their  personality traits and characteristics should be prohibited. In any case, that prohibition does not refer to or touch upon risk analytics that are not based on the profiling of individuals or on the personality traits and characteristics of individuals, such as AI systems using risk  analytics  to  assess  the  likelihood  of  financial  fraud  by  undertakings  on  the  basis  of  suspicious  transactions  or risk analytic tools to predict the likelihood of the localisation of narcotics or illicit goods by customs authorities, for example on the basis  of  known trafficking  routes.

Based on the provided context from the GDPR regulation, the following categories of personal data are relevant for the processing activity **"Credit assessment"** for the purpose of **"Obtaining information on the creditworthiness or solvency of (future) customers"**:

### **Relevant Data Categories (from the context):**
1. **Economic Situation** (as defined in the definition of *profiling* under Article 4(4)):
   - This includes financial data such as income, debt levels, credit history, payment behavior, and financial transactions.
   - The context explicitly mentions **"creditworthiness"** and **"credit score"** as key elements in AI systems used for this purpose (see Section 5(b)).

2. **Behavioral Data** (as part of *profiling* under Article 4(4)):
   - This includes data on past financial behavior (e.g., loan repayment history, defaults, late payments).
   - The context implies that automated processing (e.g., AI-driven credit scoring) relies on such data to evaluate economic reliability.

3. **Identifiers & Location Data** (as part of *personal data* under Article 4(1)):
   - Names, identification numbers, or online identifiers (e.g., bank account details, tax IDs) may be used to link financial records to individuals.
   - Location data (e.g., residency for credit checks) could also be relevant in some cases.

### **Additional Notes (from the context):**
- The processing of such data for credit assessment falls under **Article 5(b)**, which explicitly regulates AI systems used to evaluate creditworthiness (with exceptions for fraud detection).
- The **definition of profiling** (Article 4(4)) confirms that automated processing of personal data to evaluate economic aspects (e.g., creditworthiness) is covered.
- The **legal basis** for such processing would typically be **Article 6(1)(b) (contractual necessity)** or **Article 6(1)(f) (legitimate interest)**, though the context does not specify this.

### **Missing Information (not covered in the provided context):**
- Specific examples of data points (e.g., salary history, outstanding loans, credit reports).
- Explicit legal bases for processing (though the context implies contractual necessity or legitimate interest).
- Details on data subject rights (e.g., right to explanation for automated decisions under Article 22).

### **Conclusion:**
The most relevant categories are **economic situation, behavioral data, and identifiers/location data**, as they directly relate to creditworthiness assessment under the provided GDPR context.

results.output:
Based on the provided context from the GDPR regulation, the categories of personal data relevant for the processing activity **"Management" for the purpose: "Recording and documentation of internal expenses for employee catering costs"** would likely include the following:

### **Relevant Personal Data Categories:**
1. **Identifying Data** (Article 4(1)):
   - **Name** (of the employee submitting the expense claim).
   - **Employee ID or other identifiers** (e.g., staff number, email address).
   - **Location data** (if relevant, e.g., department or cost center for allocation).

2. **Financial Data** (related to "expenses"):
   - **Amount spent** (on catering).
   - **Date and time** of the expense (if linked to an individual).
   - **Vendor/merchant details** (if the expense is tied to a specific transaction involving the employee).

3. **Employment-Related Data** (Article 88 - Processing in the context of employment):
   - **Department/team** (for cost allocation).
   - **Employment status** (e.g., full-time, part-time, contractor) if relevant to reimbursement policies.

### **Special Categories of Data (Article 9(1)):**
- **Not applicable** in this context, as catering expenses do not inherently involve sensitive data (e.g., health, biometric, or racial/ethnic origin). However, if the expense includes **meal preferences due to dietary restrictions (e.g., medical allergies)**, this could potentially fall under **health-related data** (a special category). If so, explicit consent or another lawful basis (e.g., legal obligation under employment law) would be required.

### **Missing Context (Not Addressed in Provided GDPR Excerpts):**
- **Purpose Limitation (Article 5(1)(b))**: The processing must be "adequate, relevant, and limited to what is necessary" for the stated purpose (recording expenses). If additional data (e.g., dietary habits) is collected beyond what is necessary, it would violate GDPR.
- **Lawful Basis (Article 6)**: The legal basis for processing could be:
  - **Contractual necessity** (if reimbursement is part of the employment contract).
  - **Legitimate interest** (if the employer has a valid reason, e.g., cost control, and balances it against employee rights).
  - **Legal obligation** (if tax or accounting laws require expense documentation).
- **Retention Periods (Article 5(1)(e))**: The context does not specify how long data should be kept. Typically, expense records are retained for tax/audit purposes (e.g., 6–10 years, depending on national law).
- **Security Measures (Article 32)**: The context mentions technical/organizational measures (e.g., encryption, access controls) but does not detail them.

### **Conclusion:**
The core personal data categories for this processing activity are **identifying data (name/ID), financial data (amount/vendor), and employment-related data (department)**. If dietary restrictions are involved, **health data (a special category)** may apply, requiring stricter safeguards. The GDPR principles of **purpose limitation, data minimization, and lawful basis** must be adhered to.

Based on the provided GDPR context, the relevant categories of personal data for the processing activity **"Performance of payroll accounting"** for the purpose **"Calculation of monthly salaries in the HR department"** would include:

### **Relevant Personal Data Categories:**
1. **Identifying Data** (as per the definition of "personal data" in the context):
   - Name (direct identifier)
   - Identification number (e.g., employee ID, tax ID, social security number)
   - Location data (e.g., work location, if relevant for payroll)
   - Online identifiers (e.g., company email, if used for payroll purposes)

2. **Employment-Related Data** (as implied by the context of payroll processing):
   - Employment contract details (e.g., salary, bonuses, deductions)
   - Bank account details (for salary payments)
   - Tax-related information (e.g., tax identification number, tax class)
   - Working hours or attendance records (if relevant for salary calculation)
   - Benefits or allowances (e.g., health insurance, pension contributions)

3. **Special Categories of Data (if applicable)**:
   - Health-related data (e.g., sick leave records, if used for salary adjustments)
   - Trade union membership (if relevant for payroll deductions)

### **Legal Basis for Processing (from the context):**
- **Article 88 (Processing in the context of employment)** allows Member States to provide specific rules for payroll processing, ensuring safeguards for employees' rights.
- **Article 6(1)(b) GDPR** (implied) – Processing is necessary for the performance of a contract (employment contract) and compliance with legal obligations (e.g., tax and social security laws).
- **Article 6(1)(c) GDPR** – Processing is necessary for compliance with a legal obligation (e.g., tax reporting).

### **Missing Information (not provided in the context):**
- Whether **biometric data** (e.g., fingerprints for time tracking) is involved.
- Whether **genetic or health data** is processed beyond standard payroll needs.
- Specific **Member State laws** governing payroll processing (as per Article 88).

### **Conclusion:**
The primary personal data categories for payroll accounting are **identifying data, employment-related data, and possibly special categories if health or union-related deductions are involved**. The legal basis is typically **contractual necessity (Article 6(1)(b))** and **legal obligation (Article 6(1)(c))**, with additional safeguards under **Article 88** for employment-related processing.

In  that  context,  'public  health'  should  be interpreted  as  defined  in  Regulation  (EC)  No  1338/2008  of  the  European  Parliament  and  of  the  Council  ( 1 ), namely  all  elements  related  to  health,  namely  health  status,  including  morbidity  and  disability,  the  determinants having  an  effect  on  that  health  status,  health  care  needs,  resources  allocated  to  health  care,  the  provision  of,  and universal access to, health care as well as health care expenditure and financing, and the causes of mortality. Such processing  of  data  concerning  health  for  reasons  of  public  interest  should  not  result  in  personal  data  being processed for other purposes by third parties such as employers or insurance and banking companies. - (55) Moreover, the processing of personal data by official authorities for  the purpose of achieving the aims, laid down by  constitutional  law  or  by  international  public  law,  of  officially  recognised  religious  associations,  is  carried  out on grounds of public interest. - (56) Where  in  the  course  of  electoral  activities,  the  operation  of  the  democratic  system  in  a  Member  State  requires that  political  parties  compile  personal  data  on  people's  political  opinions,  the  processing  of  such  data  may  be permitted for reasons of public interest, provided that appropriate safeguards are established.

A  derogation  should  also  allow  the  processing  of  such personal  data  where  necessary  for  the  establishment,  exercise  or  defence  of legal  claims,  whether  in  court proceedings or in an administrative or out-of-court procedure.

- (53) Special categories of personal data which merit higher  protection should be processed for health-related purposes only  where  necessary  to  achieve  those  purposes  for  the  benefit  of  natural  persons  and  society  as  a  whole,  in particular  in  the  context  of  the  management  of  health  or  social  care  services  and  systems,  including  processing by  the  management  and  central  national  health  authorities  of  such  data  for  the  purpose  of  quality  control, management information and the  general national  and  local  supervision  of  the  health  or  social  care  system,  and ensuring  continuity  of  health  or  social  care  and  cross-border  healthcare  or  health  security,  monitoring  and  alert purposes,  or  for  archiving  purposes  in  the  public  interest,  scientific  or  historical  research  purposes  or  statistical purposes,  based  on  Union  or  Member  State  law  which  has  to  meet  an  objective  of  public  interest,  as  well  as  for studies  conducted in the public interest in the area of public health.

Therefore, this Regulation should provide for harmonised  conditions  for  the  processing  of  special  categories  of  personal  data  concerning  health,  in  respect  of specific  needs,  in  particular  where  the  processing  of  such  data  is  carried  out  for  certain  health-related  purposes

- (51) Personal  data  which  are,  by  their  nature,  particularly  sensitive  in  relation  to  fundamental  rights  and  freedoms merit specific protection as the context of  their  processing could create significant risks to the fundamental rights and freedoms. Those personal data should include personal data revealing racial or ethnic origin, whereby the use of  the  term  'racial  origin'  in  this  Regulation  does  not  imply  an  acceptance  by  the  Union  of  theories  which attempt to determine the existence of separate human races. The processing of photographs should not systemati­ cally  be  considered to be processing of special categories of personal data as they are covered by the definition of biometric  data  only  when  processed  through  a  specific  technical  means  allowing  the  unique  identification  or authentication  of  a  natural  person.  Such  personal  data  should  not  be  processed,  unless  processing  is  allowed  in specific  cases  set  out  in  this  Regulation,  taking  into  account  that  Member  States  law  may  lay  down  specific provisions  on  data  protection  in  order  to  adapt  the  application  of  the  rules  of  this  Regulation  for  compliance with  a  legal  obligation  or  for  the  performance  of  a  task  carried  out  in  the  public  interest  or  in  the  exercise  of official  authority  vested in  the  controller.  In  addition  to the  specific  requirements  for  such processing,  the  general principles  and  other  rules  of  this  Regulation  should  apply,  in  particular  as  regards  the  conditions  for  lawful processing.

- (1)  'personal  data'  means  any  information  relating  to  an  identified  or  identifiable  natural  person  ('data  subject');  an identifiable  natural  person  is  one  who  can  be  identified,  directly  or  indirectly,  in  particular  by  reference  to  an identifier  such  as  a  name,  an  identification  number,  location  data,  an  online  identifier  or  to  one  or  more  factors specific to the physical, physiological, genetic, mental, economic, cultural or social identity of that natural person; - (2)  'processing'  means  any  operation  or  set  of  operations  which  is  performed  on  personal  data  or  on  sets  of  personal data,  whether  or not by  automated  means,  such  as  collection,  recording,  organisation,  structuring, storage, adaptation or alteration,  retrieval,  consultation,  use,  disclosure  by  transmission,  dissemination  or  otherwise  making available, alignment or combination, restriction, erasure or destruction; - (3)  'restriction  of  processing'  means  the  marking  of  stored  personal  data  with  the  aim  of  limiting  their  processing  in the future; - (4)  'profiling'  means  any  form  of  automated  processing  of  personal  data  consisting  of  the  use  of  personal  data  to evaluate certain personal aspects relating to a natural person, in particular  to analyse or  predict aspects concerning that  natural  person's  performance  at  work,  economic  situation,  health,  personal  preferences,  interests,  reliability, behaviour, location or movements;

In  addition  to the  specific  requirements  for  such processing,  the  general principles  and  other  rules  of  this  Regulation  should  apply,  in  particular  as  regards  the  conditions  for  lawful processing.  Derogations  from  the  general  prohibition  for  processing  such  special  categories  of  personal  data should  be  explicitly  provided,  inter  alia,  where  the  data  subject  gives  his  or  her  explicit  consent  or  in  respect  of specific  needs  in  particular  where  the  processing  is  carried  out  in  the  course  of  legitimate  activities  by  certain associations or foundations the purpose of which is to permit the exercise of fundamental freedoms. - (52) Derogating  from  the  prohibition  on  processing  special  categories  of  personal  data  should  also  be  allowed  when provided for  in  Union or  Member State law and subject to suitable safeguards, so as to protect personal data and other  fundamental  rights,  where  it  is  in  the  public  interest  to  do  so,  in  particular  processing  personal  data  in  the field  of  employment  law,  social  protection  law  including  pensions  and  for  health  security,  monitoring  and  alert purposes,  the  prevention  or  control  of  communicable  diseases  and  other  serious  threats  to  health.  Such  a derogation  may  be  made  for  health  purposes,  including  public  health  and  the  management  of  health-care services,  especially  in  order  to  ensure  the  quality  and  cost-effectiveness  of  the  procedures  used  for  settling  claims for  benefits  and  services  in  the  health  insurance  system,  or  for  archiving  purposes  in  the  public  interest,  scientific or  historical  research  purposes  or  statistical  purposes.

Processing  of  personal  data

based  on  the  vital  interest  of  another  natural  person  should  in  principle  take  place  only  where  the  processing cannot  be  manifestly  based  on  another  legal  basis.  Some  types  of  processing  may  serve  both  important  grounds of  public  interest  and  the  vital  interests  of  the  data  subject  as  for  instance  when  processing  is  necessary  for humanitarian  purposes,  including  for  monitoring  epidemics  and  their  spread  or  in  situations  of  humanitarian emergencies, in particular in situations of natural and man-made disasters.

3. 4. Member States  may  maintain  or  introduce  further  conditions,  including  limitations,  with  regard  to  the  processing of genetic data, biometric data or data concerning health.

## Article 10

## Processing of personal data relating to criminal convictions and offences

Processing  of  personal  data  relating  to  criminal  convictions  and  offences  or related  security  measures  based  on Article  6(1)  shall  be  carried  out  only  under  the  control  of  official  authority  or  when  the  processing  is  authorised  by Union  or  Member  State  law  providing  for  appropriate  safeguards  for  the  rights  and  freedoms  of  data  subjects.  Any comprehensive register of criminal convictions shall be kept only under  the control of official authority.

## Article 11

## Processing which does not require identification

- (35) Personal  data  concerning  health  should  include  all  data  pertaining  to  the  health  status  of  a  data  subject  which reveal information relating to the past, current or  future physical or  mental health status of  the data subject.

This includes  information  about  the  natural  person  collected  in  the  course  of  the  registration  for,  or  the  provision  of, health  care  services  as  referred  to  in  Directive  2011/24/EU  of  the  European  Parliament  and  of  the  Council  ( 1 )  to that  natural  person;  a  number,  symbol  or  particular  assigned  to  a  natural  person  to  uniquely  identify  the  natural person  for  health  purposes;  information  derived  from  the  testing  or  examination  of  a  body  part  or  bodily substance,  including  from  genetic  data  and  biological  samples;  and  any  information  on,  for  example,  a  disease, disability,  disease  risk,  medical  history,  clinical  treatment  or  the  physiological  or  biomedical  state  of  the  data subject independent of its source, for example from a physician or other health professional, a hospital, a medical device or an in vitro diagnostic test.

(b) as  regards  a  processor  with  establishments  in  more  than  one  Member  State,  the  place  of  its  central  adminis­ tration  in  the  Union,  or,  if  the  processor  has  no  central  administration  in  the  Union,  the  establishment  of  the processor  in  the  Union where  the  main processing activities  in  the  context of  the  activities  of  an  establishment of  the  processor  take  place  to  the  extent  that  the  processor  is  subject  to  specific  obligations  under  this Regulation; - (17)  'representative'  means  a  natural  or  legal  person  established  in  the  Union  who,  designated  by  the  controller  or processor  in  writing  pursuant  to  Article  27,  represents  the  controller  or  processor  with  regard  to  their  respective obligations under  this Regulation; - (18)  'enterprise'  means a natural or legal person engaged in an economic activity, irrespective of its legal form, including partnerships or associations regularly engaged in an economic activity; - (19)  'group of undertakings' means a controlling undertaking and its controlled undertakings;
results.output:
Based on the provided GDPR context, the relevant categories of personal data for the processing activity **"Recording of patient findings"** for the purpose of **"Processing of patient data for treatment, billing and transmission via a practice management system or other systems"** are as follows:

### **Relevant Data Categories (from GDPR Context):**
1. **Personal Data (Article 4(1))**
   - Any information relating to an identified or identifiable natural person (e.g., name, identification number, location data, online identifier, or factors specific to their physical, physiological, genetic, mental, economic, cultural, or social identity).

2. **Data Concerning Health (Article 4(15))**
   - Personal data related to the **physical or mental health** of a natural person, including:
     - Health status (past, current, or future).
     - Information collected during registration or provision of healthcare services.
     - Unique identifiers for health purposes (e.g., patient ID numbers).
     - Data derived from testing or examination of body parts or bodily substances (including genetic data and biological samples).
     - Information on diseases, disabilities, medical history, clinical treatment, or physiological/biomedical state.

3. **Special Categories of Personal Data (Article 9(1))**
   - **Health data** (as defined above) is explicitly listed as a special category requiring higher protection.
   - Other special categories (e.g., racial/ethnic origin, biometric data) may apply if relevant to the processing context.

### **Legal Basis for Processing (Article 6 & 9):**
- **Article 6(1)(b)** – Necessary for the performance of a contract (e.g., treatment agreement).
- **Article 6(1)(c)** – Necessary for compliance with a legal obligation (e.g., billing requirements).
- **Article 6(1)(e)** – Necessary for the performance of a task carried out in the public interest (e.g., healthcare system management).
- **Article 9(2)(h)** – Necessary for the purposes of preventive or occupational medicine, medical diagnosis, or the provision of healthcare (subject to safeguards).
- **Article 9(2)(i)** – Necessary for reasons of public interest in public health (e.g., ensuring quality and safety of healthcare).

### **Additional Considerations:**
- **Professional Secrecy (Article 9(3))**: If processed by a healthcare professional bound by confidentiality, this provides an additional safeguard.
- **Transmission via Practice Management Systems**: Must comply with GDPR principles (lawfulness, fairness, transparency, purpose limitation, data minimization, accuracy, storage limitation, integrity, and confidentiality).

### **Missing Information (Not Provided in Context):**
- Specific examples of **patient findings** (e.g., diagnoses, lab results, imaging data).
- Details on **data recipients** (e.g., insurers, third-party billing services).
- Whether **explicit consent** is obtained (if applicable under national law).

### **Conclusion:**
The primary relevant data categories are **health data** (special category) and **general personal data**, processed under **Article 6 and 9 GDPR** for treatment, billing, and system transmission, with appropriate safeguards.
results.ground_truth: billing data, address data, treatment data, health data (special category pursuant to Art. 9 GDPR), name, social security number

## Article 8

## Conditions applicable to child's consent in relation to information society services

1. Where  point  (a)  of  Article  6(1)  applies,  in  relation  to  the  offer  of  information  society  services  directly  to  a  child, the  processing  of  the  personal  data  of  a  child  shall  be  lawful  where  the  child  is  at  least  16  years  old.  Where  the  child  is below  the  age  of  16  years,  such  processing  shall  be  lawful  only  if  and  to  the  extent  that  consent  is  given  or  authorised by the holder of parental responsibility over  the child.

Member  States  may  provide  by  law  for  a  lower  age  for  those  purposes  provided  that  such  lower  age  is  not  below  13 years.

2. The  controller  shall  make  reasonable  efforts  to  verify  in  such  cases  that  consent  is  given  or  authorised  by  the holder of parental responsibility over  the child, taking into consideration available technology.
3. Paragraph 1 shall  not  affect  the  general  contract  law of  Member  States  such  as  the  rules  on  the  validity,  formation or effect of a contract in relation to a child.

## Article 9

## Processing of special categories of personal data

1. Personal  data  should  be  processed  in  a manner  that  ensures  appropriate  security  and  confidentiality  of  the  personal  data,  including  for  preventing unauthorised access to or use of personal data and the equipment used for the processing.

Such  legitimate  interest  could  exist for  example where there is a relevant  and appropriate relationship  between the  data subject  and the controller  in situations such as where the data subject is a client or  in the service of  the controller. At any rate the existence of a  legitimate  interest  would  need  careful  assessment  including  whether  a  data  subject  can  reasonably expect  at  the time  and  in  the  context  of  the  collection  of  the  personal  data  that  processing  for  that  purpose  may  take  place. The  interests  and  fundamental  rights  of  the  data  subject  could  in  particular  override  the  interest  of  the  data controller  where  personal  data  are  processed  in  circumstances  where  data  subjects  do  not  reasonably  expect further  processing.  Given  that  it  is  for  the  legislator  to  provide  by  law  for  the  legal  basis  for  public  authorities  to process personal data, that legal basis should not apply to the processing by public authorities in the performance  of  their  tasks.  The  processing  of  personal  data  strictly  necessary  for  the  purposes  of  preventing fraud  also  constitutes  a  legitimate  interest  of  the  data  controller  concerned.  The  processing  of  personal  data  for direct marketing purposes may be regarded as carried out for a legitimate interest.

Processing  of  personal  data

Based on the provided GDPR context, the categories of personal data relevant for the processing activity **"Guest Wi-Fi / network access"** for the purpose **"Provision of an Internet connection via Wi-Fi or network cable for guests, customers or other visitors to the business premises"** are as follows:

### **Relevant Data Categories:**
1. **Online Identifiers** (e.g., IP addresses, MAC addresses, device identifiers, or cookie identifiers)
   - *Rationale*: The context explicitly mentions that online identifiers (e.g., IP addresses, cookie identifiers) can be used to identify natural persons, especially when combined with other data (Recital 30). Guest Wi-Fi/network access inherently involves processing such identifiers to establish and manage connections.

2. **Location Data** (if applicable, e.g., tracking Wi-Fi access points or network usage)
   - *Rationale*: Location data is explicitly listed as an identifier under the definition of personal data (Article 4(1)).

3. **Other Identifiers** (e.g., login credentials, usernames, or device names if required for network access)
   - *Rationale*: While not explicitly listed in the provided context, these would fall under the broad definition of personal data as "any information relating to an identified or identifiable natural person" (Article 4(1)).

### **Special Categories of Personal Data (Not Applicable Unless Explicitly Processed):**
- The context does not indicate that the business processes **special categories of personal data** (e.g., racial/ethnic origin, health data, biometric data, etc.) as part of guest Wi-Fi/network access. However, if such data were collected (e.g., via a mandatory login requiring sensitive information), they would fall under **Article 9** and require explicit justification (e.g., consent, legal obligation, or public interest).

### **Missing Context (Not Addressed in Provided Text):**
- The context does not specify whether **logging of network activity** (e.g., browsing history, timestamps) is part of the processing. If such data is collected, it would also qualify as personal data under the broad definition.
- The context does not address **retention periods** or **security measures** for guest Wi-Fi data, which are critical under GDPR (e.g., encryption, access controls).

### **Conclusion:**
The primary categories of personal data processed in this scenario are **online identifiers** (e.g., IP/MAC addresses) and potentially **location data** or **other identifiers** (e.g., device names). No special categories of data are inherently involved unless explicitly collected. The processing must comply with GDPR principles, including lawful basis (e.g., legitimate interest under Article 6(1)(f) or consent), transparency, data minimization, and security measures.

Categories of data:
retriever_context: - (1)  'personal  data'  means  any  information  relating  to  an  identified  or  identifiable  natural  person  ('data  subject');  an identifiable  natural  person  is  one  who  can  be  identified,  directly  or  indirectly,  in  particular  by  reference  to  an identifier  such  as  a  name,  an  identification  number,  location  data,  an  online  identifier  or  to  one  or  more  factors specific to the physical, physiological, genetic, mental, economic, cultural or social identity of that natural person; - (2)  'processing'  means  any  operation  or  set  of  operations  which  is  performed  on  personal  data  or  on  sets  of  personal data,  whether  or not by  automated  means,  such  as  collection,  recording,  organisation,  structuring, storage, adaptation or alteration,  retrieval,  consultation,  use,  disclosure  by  transmission,  dissemination  or  otherwise  making available, alignment or combination, restriction, erasure or destruction; - (3)  'restriction  of  processing'  means  the  marking  of  stored  personal  data  with  the  aim  of  limiting  their  processing  in the future; - (4)  'profiling'  means  any  form  of  automated  processing  of  personal  data  consisting  of  the  use  of  personal  data  to evaluate certain personal aspects relating to a natural person, in particular  to analyse or  predict aspects concerning that  natural  person's  performance  at  work,  economic  situation,  health,  personal  preferences,  interests,  reliability, behaviour, location or movements;

- (51) Personal  data  which  are,  by  their  nature,  particularly  sensitive  in  relation  to  fundamental  rights  and  freedoms merit specific protection as the context of  their  processing could create significant risks to the fundamental rights and freedoms. Those personal data should include personal data revealing racial or ethnic origin, whereby the use of  the  term  'racial  origin'  in  this  Regulation  does  not  imply  an  acceptance  by  the  Union  of  theories  which attempt to determine the existence of separate human races. The processing of photographs should not systemati­ cally  be  considered to be processing of special categories of personal data as they are covered by the definition of biometric  data  only  when  processed  through  a  specific  technical  means  allowing  the  unique  identification  or authentication  of  a  natural  person.  Such  personal  data  should  not  be  processed,  unless  processing  is  allowed  in specific  cases  set  out  in  this  Regulation,  taking  into  account  that  Member  States  law  may  lay  down  specific provisions  on  data  protection  in  order  to  adapt  the  application  of  the  rules  of  this  Regulation  for  compliance with  a  legal  obligation  or  for  the  performance  of  a  task  carried  out  in  the  public  interest  or  in  the  exercise  of official  authority  vested in  the  controller. results.output:
Based on the provided GDPR context, the categories of personal data relevant for the processing activity **"Training" for the purpose of "Providing training to raise awareness or increase the expertise of employees"** are as follows:

### **Relevant Categories of Personal Data:**
1. **General Personal Data** (Article 4(1)):
   - Any information relating to an **identified or identifiable natural person** (employee), such as:
     - **Name** (direct identifier)
     - **Employee ID or identification number** (indirect identifier)
     - **Contact details** (e.g., email, phone number)
     - **Job title, department, or role** (professional identity)
     - **Performance-related data** (e.g., training attendance, assessments, or feedback)
     - **Employment records** (e.g., contract details, work history)
     - **Biometric data** (if used for authentication, e.g., fingerprint scans for training access, but only if processed via **specific technical means** for identification—otherwise, photographs alone are not automatically biometric data under GDPR).

2. **Special Categories of Personal Data (Article 9 & Recital 51-52)**:
   - **Health data** (if training involves medical or health-related assessments, e.g., ergonomic training requiring health checks).
   - **Data revealing racial or ethnic origin** (if training addresses diversity, inclusion, or anti-discrimination topics).
   - **Genetic or biometric data** (if used for authentication in training systems, e.g., facial recognition for access control).
   - **Data concerning health or social care** (if training includes mental health or well-being programs).
   - **Data on philosophical or religious beliefs** (if training covers workplace ethics or values).

   **Note:** Processing special categories requires a **lawful basis** (e.g., explicit consent, substantial public interest, or employment law derogations under **Article 88** or **Article 9(2)(h)** for health-related training).

3. **Employment-Related Data (Article 88, Recital 155-156)**:
   - Data processed **in the employment context** (e.g., training records, performance metrics, or disciplinary actions) may fall under **Member State laws or collective agreements** governing workplace data processing.

4. **Profiling Data (Article 4(4))**:
   - If training involves **automated assessments** (e.g., AI-driven training modules that analyze employee performance), this could constitute **profiling** (e.g., tracking progress, predicting training needs).

---

### **Key GDPR Principles Applicable:**
1. **Lawful Basis (Article 6)**:
   - **Consent** (if employees voluntarily participate in training).
   - **Performance of a contract** (if training is mandatory for employment).
   - **Legal obligation** (if training is required by law, e.g., health & safety).
   - **Legitimate interests** (if training is for business purposes, balancing against employee rights).

Based on the provided GDPR context, the storage limitation principle (Article 5(1)(e)) states that personal data must be kept in a form that permits identification of data subjects for no longer than necessary for the purposes for which they are processed.

For the specific processing activity of **"Checking and documenting your 3G (recovered, vaccinated or tested) status" in the workplace**, the following applies:

### **Key Considerations:**
1. **Purpose Limitation (Article 5(1)(b))**:
   - The data must be collected for a specific, explicit, and legitimate purpose (e.g., ensuring workplace safety).
   - Once the purpose is fulfilled (e.g., the pandemic emergency subsides or workplace safety measures no longer require 3G checks), the data should be deleted unless an exception applies.

2. **Storage Limitation (Article 5(1)(e))**:
   - The data should not be stored longer than necessary.
   - If the 3G regulation is no longer legally required (e.g., due to changes in public health guidance or national law), the data must be deleted.
   - If the employer continues to process the data for a different lawful purpose (e.g., archiving for legal claims), the storage must comply with **Article 89(1)** (archiving in the public interest, scientific/historical research, or statistical purposes) and include appropriate safeguards.

3. **Legal Basis (Article 6)**:
   - If the processing is based on **public interest (Article 6(1)(e))** (e.g., workplace safety regulations), the employer must ensure compliance with national laws (e.g., under **Article 88**, which allows Member States to set specific rules for employment-related processing).
   - If the processing is based on **consent (Article 6(1)(a))**, the data subject can withdraw consent at any time, requiring deletion unless another legal basis applies.

4. **Exceptions for Extended Storage (Article 5(1)(e) & Article 89(1))**:
   - If the employer retains the data for **archiving, scientific/historical research, or statistical purposes**, they must:
     - Implement **appropriate technical/organisational measures** (e.g., pseudonymisation).
     - Ensure the storage is **proportionate** and **limited to what is necessary**.
     - Comply with **Article 89(1)** safeguards.

### **Conclusion:**
- **Default Rule**: The 3G status data should be **deleted as soon as it is no longer necessary** for the purpose of workplace safety checks (e.g., when the legal requirement for 3G checks ends).
- **Exceptions**:
  - If national law (under **Article 88**) or a collective agreement allows retention for a **specific employment-related purpose** (e.g., legal claims, health & safety records), the data may be stored longer but must still comply with **storage limitation**.
  - If retained for **archiving/research purposes**, it must meet **Article 89(1)** conditions.

### **Missing Information:**
- The exact **national law or collective agreement** governing workplace 3G checks (under **Article 88**) is not provided, which could specify retention periods.
- The **end date of the 3G regulation** (if it has been lifted) is not mentioned, which would determine when deletion is required.

**Final Answer:**
Personal data collected for "Checking and documenting your 3G status" in the workplace must be **deleted as soon as it is no longer necessary** for the purpose of workplace safety checks, unless:
1. A **legal obligation** (e.g., national law under **Article 88**) requires retention for a specific employment-related purpose (e.g., health & safety records), or
2. The data is retained for **archiving, scientific/historical research, or statistical purposes** under **Article 89(1)**, with appropriate safeguards.

Without specific national law details, the **default position is deletion once the 3G requirement is no longer legally mandated**.

results.output:
Based on the provided GDPR context, the storage limitation principle (Article 5(1)(e)) states that personal data must be kept in a form that permits identification of data subjects for no longer than necessary for the purposes for which they are processed.

For the processing activity **"Absence time recording"** with the purpose **"Documentation of employee absences and absence times"**, the following applies:

1. **General Rule (Article 5(1)(e))**:
   - Personal data must be deleted once they are no longer necessary for the purpose of documenting employee absences.
   - The controller must establish time limits for erasure or periodic review to ensure compliance.

2. **Employment Context (Recital 155)**:
   - Member State law or collective agreements may provide specific rules for processing employee data, including retention periods.
   - If such rules exist (e.g., national labor laws requiring retention for a certain period for legal or accounting purposes), they would take precedence.

3. **Archiving Exceptions (Article 5(1)(e) & Recital 156)**:
   - If the data are stored for **archiving in the public interest, scientific/historical research, or statistical purposes**, they may be kept longer, but only if:
     - Appropriate technical/organizational safeguards (e.g., pseudonymization) are implemented.
     - The controller assesses whether the purpose can be fulfilled with anonymized data.

### **Conclusion**:
- **Default Rule**: Personal data for absence time recording must be deleted once no longer needed for documenting absences (e.g., after the employment relationship ends, unless legal retention obligations apply).
- **If Legal Retention Applies**: If national law requires retention (e.g., for tax or labor law compliance), the data may be kept for the legally mandated period.
- **If Used for Research/Archiving**: If repurposed for research/archiving, stricter safeguards (e.g., pseudonymization) and compliance with Article 89(1) are required.

**Missing Context**:
- The exact retention period under national employment or financial services law (if applicable).
- Whether the data are anonymized/pseudonymized for archiving purposes.

**Final Answer**:
Personal data for absence time recording must be deleted once no longer necessary for documenting absences, unless:
1. A legal retention obligation (e.g., under national law) applies, or
2. The data are repurposed for archiving/research with appropriate safeguards (Article 89(1)).

The controller must establish clear time limits for erasure or review.

For the processing activity **"Appointment of an agency to provide services"**, the following applies:

1. **Default Rule**: Personal data must be deleted **once the purpose is fulfilled** (i.e., after the agency has been appointed and its services provided). The data should not be retained beyond what is necessary for the original purpose.

2. **Exceptions for Extended Storage**:
   - If the data is stored **solely for archiving, scientific/historical research, or statistical purposes** (Article 89(1)), it may be kept longer, but only if:
     - Appropriate technical/organizational safeguards are implemented.
     - The storage is in the **public interest** or for research/statistical purposes.
   - If required by **Union or Member State law** (e.g., legal obligations, public interest tasks).

3. **Right to Erasure (Article 17)**:
   - If the data subject withdraws consent or objects to processing, the data must be erased unless an exception applies (e.g., legal compliance, public interest).

### Missing Information:

  - Any applicable national laws requiring retention.

### Conclusion:
**Personal data for "Appointment of an agency to provide services" must generally be deleted once the purpose is fulfilled, unless an exception (e.g., legal obligation, archiving) applies.** Without additional context, the default is deletion upon completion of the service.

results.output:
Based on the provided GDPR context, the storage limitation principle (Article 5(1)(e)) states that personal data must be kept in a form that permits identification of data subjects for **no longer than is necessary** for the purposes for which they are processed.

For the processing activity **"Preparation of offers"** with the purpose **"Obtaining offers to determine the best conditions"**, the following applies:

1. **Storage Limitation Principle**:
   - Personal data must be deleted or anonymized once the purpose ("obtaining offers to determine the best conditions") is fulfilled.
   - The data should not be retained longer than strictly necessary for this purpose.

2. **No Explicit Retention Period**:
   - The context does not specify a fixed retention period for this specific purpose.
   - The controller must establish a **time limit** for erasure or periodic review (as mentioned in Article 5(1)(e) and the transparency principle in Article 6).

3. **Key Considerations**:
   - If the data is no longer needed for determining the best conditions (e.g., after the offer is accepted, rejected, or the negotiation is concluded), it should be deleted.
   - If the data is needed for **archiving, scientific research, or legal claims** (as per Article 5(1)(e) and Article 89), longer retention may be justified, but this must be documented with appropriate safeguards.

### **Conclusion**:
The personal data for **"Preparation of offers"** must be deleted **once the purpose ("obtaining offers to determine the best conditions") is fulfilled**, unless an exception (e.g., legal obligation, archiving) applies. The controller must define a **specific retention period** based on necessity and document the justification.

**Missing Information**:
- No explicit retention period is provided in the context.
- No details on whether the data is used for other purposes (e.g., marketing, legal compliance) that could extend retention.

**Recommendation**:
The controller should define a **clear retention policy** (e.g., delete after 30 days if no response) and document the legal basis for any extended storage.

- (40) In  order  for  processing  to  be  lawful,  personal  data  should  be  processed  on  the  basis  of  the  consent  of  the  data subject concerned or some other legitimate basis, laid down by law, either in this Regulation or in other Union or

Member  State  law  as  referred  to  in  this  Regulation,  including  the  necessity  for  compliance  with  the  legal obligation  to which the  controller  is  subject  or  the  necessity  for  the  performance  of  a  contract  to which  the  data subject is party or in order  to take steps at the request of  the data subject prior  to entering into a contract.

After  the  completion  of  the  processing  on  behalf  of  the controller,  the  processor  should,  at  the  choice  of  the  controller,  return  or  delete  the  personal  data,  unless  there  is a requirement to store the personal data under Union or Member State law to which the processor is subject. - (82) In  order  to  demonstrate  compliance  with  this  Regulation,  the  controller  or  processor  should  maintain  records  of processing  activities  under  its  responsibility.  Each  controller  and  processor  should  be  obliged  to  cooperate  with the  supervisory  authority  and  make  those  records,  on  request,  available  to  it,  so  that  it  might serve for monitoring those processing operations. - (83) In  order  to  maintain  security  and  to  prevent  processing  in  infringement  of  this  Regulation,  the  controller  or processor  should  evaluate  the  risks  inherent  in  the  processing  and  implement  measures  to  mitigate  those  risks, such  as  encryption.  Those  measures  should  ensure  an  appropriate  level  of  security,  including  confidentiality, taking into account the state of  the art and  the costs of  implementation in relation to the risks  and  the nature of the  personal  data  to  be  protected.  In  assessing  data  security  risk,  consideration  should  be  given  to  the  risks  that are presented by personal data processing, such as accidental or unlawful destruction, loss, alteration, unauthorised  disclosure  of,  or  access  to,  personal  data  transmitted,  stored  or  otherwise  processed  which  may  in particular lead to physical, material or non-material damage.

 Where  the  data  subject makes  the  request  by  electronic  means,  and  unless  otherwise  requested  by  the  data  subject,  the  information  shall  be provided in a commonly used electronic form.

4.

The right to obtain a copy referred to in paragraph 3 shall not adversely affect the rights and freedoms of others.

## Section 3

## Rectification and erasure

## Article 16

## Right to rectification

The  data  subject  shall  have  the  right  to  obtain  from  the  controller  without  undue  delay  the  rectification  of  inaccurate personal data concerning him or her. Taking into account the purposes of  the processing, the data subject shall have the right to have incomplete personal data completed, including by means of providing a supplementary statement.

## Article 17

## Right to erasure ('right to be forgotten')

1. The data  subject  shall  have  the  right  to  obtain  from  the  controller  the  erasure  of  personal  data  concerning  him  or her  without  undue  delay  and  the  controller  shall  have  the  obligation  to  erase  personal  data  without  undue  delay  where one of the following grounds applies:
2. (a)   the  personal  data  are  no  longer  necessary  in  relation  to  the  purposes  for  which  they  were  collected  or  otherwise processed;

( 1 ) Directive (EU) 2016/680 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 27 April 2016 on the protection of natural persons with regard  to  the  processing  of  personal  data  by  competent  authorities  for  the  purposes  of  prevention,  investigation,  detection  or prosecution  of  criminal  offences  or  the  execution  of  criminal  penalties,  and  the  free  movement  of  such  data  and  repealing  Council Framework Decision 2008/977/JHA (see page 89 of this Official Journal).

( 2 ) Directive 2000/31/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council of 8 June 2000 on certain legal aspects of information society services,  in  particular  electronic  commerce,  in  the  Internal  Market  ('Directive  on  electronic  commerce')  (OJ  L  178,  17.7.2000,  p.  1).

## Article 8

## Conditions applicable to child's consent in relation to information society services

1. Where  point  (a)  of  Article  6(1)  applies,  in  relation  to  the  offer  of  information  society  services  directly  to  a  child, the  processing  of  the  personal  data  of  a  child  shall  be  lawful  where  the  child  is  at  least  16  years  old.  Where  the  child  is below  the  age  of  16  years,  such  processing  shall  be  lawful  only  if  and  to  the  extent  that  consent  is  given  or  authorised by the holder of parental responsibility over  the child.

Member  States  may  provide  by  law  for  a  lower  age  for  those  purposes  provided  that  such  lower  age  is  not  below  13 years.

2. The  controller  shall  make  reasonable  efforts  to  verify  in  such  cases  that  consent  is  given  or  authorised  by  the holder of parental responsibility over  the child, taking into consideration available technology.
3. Paragraph 1 shall  not  affect  the  general  contract  law of  Member  States  such  as  the  rules  on  the  validity,  formation or effect of a contract in relation to a child.

## Article 9

## Processing of special categories of personal data

1. Processing of personal data revealing racial or ethnic origin, political opinions, religious or  philosophical beliefs, or trade  union  membership,  and  the  processing  of  genetic  data,  biometric  data  for  the  purpose  of  uniquely  identifying  a natural  person,  data  concerning  health  or  data  concerning  a  natural  person's  sex  life  or  sexual  orientation  shall  be prohibited.

Based on the provided GDPR context, the storage limitation principle (Article 5(1)(e)) states that personal data must be kept in a form that permits identification of data subjects for **no longer than necessary** for the purposes for which they are processed.

For the processing activity **"Acceptance of orders via a web portal"** with the purpose **"Collection of customer data to initiate business processes"**, the following applies:

### **Key Considerations:**
1. **Necessity Principle (Article 5(1)(c))**:
   - Personal data should be **adequate, relevant, and limited to what is necessary** for the specified purpose.
   - If the data is no longer needed to fulfill the business process (e.g., order fulfillment, customer service, or legal obligations), it must be deleted.

2. **Storage Limitation (Article 5(1)(e))**:
   - The data must be stored **only as long as necessary** for the purpose of initiating business processes (e.g., order processing, invoicing, or legal compliance).
   - Once the business process is complete (e.g., order delivered, invoice paid), the data should be **erased or anonymized** unless another legal basis (e.g., tax retention, warranty claims) applies.

3. **Legal Obligations (Article 6(1)(c))**:
   - If there is a **legal requirement** to retain data (e.g., tax laws, accounting standards), storage may exceed the initial purpose but must still comply with GDPR storage limitation.
   - Example: Financial records may need to be kept for **6–10 years** under tax laws, but this is an exception to the general rule.

4. **Right to Erasure (Article 17)**:
   - If the data subject withdraws consent (if consent was the legal basis) or objects to processing, the data must be deleted unless another legal basis applies.

### **Conclusion:**
- **Default Rule**: Personal data collected for order acceptance must be **deleted as soon as it is no longer necessary** for business processes (e.g., after order fulfillment, unless retention is legally required).
- **Exceptions**: If retention is required by law (e.g., tax, accounting), the data may be kept for the legally mandated period, but **no longer**.

### **Missing Information (if any):**
- The exact retention period depends on **national laws** (e.g., tax, accounting, or sector-specific regulations).
- If the business has a **legitimate interest** (e.g., fraud prevention, warranty claims), retention may be extended, but this must be justified under GDPR.

## Article 6

In  particular,  a  data  subject  should  have  the  right  to  have  his  or  her  personal  data  erased and  no  longer  processed  where  the  personal  data  are  no  longer  necessary  in  relation  to  the  purposes  for  which they  are  collected  or  otherwise  processed,  where  a  data  subject  has  withdrawn  his  or  her  consent  or  objects  to the  processing  of  personal  data  concerning  him  or  her,  or  where  the  processing  of  his  or  her  personal  data  does not  otherwise  comply  with  this  Regulation.

 That  right  is  relevant  in  particular  where  the  data  subject  has  given

The  data  subject  should  be  able  to  exercise  that  right notwithstanding  the  fact  that  he  or  she  is  no  longer  a  child.  However,  the  further  retention  of  the  personal  data should  be  lawful  where  it  is  necessary,  for  exercising  the  right  of  freedom  of  expression  and  information,  for compliance  with  a  legal  obligation,  for  the  performance  of  a  task  carried  out  in  the  public  interest  or  in  the exercise  of  official  authority  vested  in  the  controller,  on  the  grounds  of  public  interest  in  the  area  of  public health,  for  archiving  purposes  in  the  public  interest,  scientific or  historical  research  purposes  or  statistical purposes, or for  the establishment, exercise or defence of  legal claims.

For  deployers  that  are  financial  institutions  subject  to  requirements  regarding  their  internal  governance,  arrangements  or processes under Union financial services law, the monitoring obligation set out in the first subparagraph shall be deemed to be fulfilled by complying with the rules on internal governance arrangements, processes and mechanisms pursuant to the relevant  financial  service  law.
